coredata.dc:title: Carbon-in-carbon: Hybrid carbonized nanomaterials with multifunctional activities for the treatment of endophthalmitis

Chiou, Yi-Ru

Chen, Chien-Fu

Lai, Jui-Yang
Huang, Chih-Ching
Chang, Huan-Tsung
coredata.dc:description: Infectious endophthalmitis (EO) is a severe eye condition characterized by inflammation of the eye’s internal fluids and tissues, often resulting from infection by bacteria or fungi. Patients with EO typically experience reduced vision, pain, and conjunctival infection. Despite the use of antibiotics, challenges such as inflammation and drug resistance remain. In this work, we have developed dextran/aliphatic diamines carbonized nanogels (DEX/ADA-CNGs) as board-spectrum antimicrobial agents by a one-step mild carbonization procedure. The CNGs exhibit not only antimicrobial properties but also show good drug delivery potential. Curcumin-based carbon quantum dots (Cur-CQDs) as an anti-inflammatory agent are loaded into DEX/ADA-CNGs to form hybrid carbon-in-carbon nanocomposites (Cur-CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs), showing combined antimicrobial, antioxidative, and anti-inflammatory activities. Impressively, CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs maintain strong antibacterial effectiveness against drug-resistant strains, with minimum inhibitory concentrations below 110 µg mL−1. The Cur-CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs have demonstrated high biocompatibility both in vitro and in vivo. In a rat model of EO, they rapidly (less than 5 days) reduce microbial infection and inflammation in the eye. Given these promising results, Cur-CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs show great potential as an enduring intravitreal treatment option for infectious EO.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of DEX/ADA-CNGs. (A) UV–vis absorption spectra, (B) fluorescence spectra, (C) FTIR spectra, and (D) TEM images of DEX/ADA-CNGs (i.e., DEX70k/DA(1,10)-CNGs), Cur-CQDs, and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs. The materials were diluted to 250 μg mL−1 with ultrapure water for optical spectral measurements. The insets in (D) are their corresponding size distribution histogram and HR-TEM images.

Evaluation of antibacterial activities of Cur-CQDs, DEX/ADA-CNGs, Cur@DEX/ADA-CNGs, and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs. (A) Photographs of agar plates showing the number of colonies of S. aureus and E. coli (a) without and with treatments of Cur-CQDs, DEX/ADA-CNGs, Cur@DEX/ADA-CNGs, and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs (100 μg mL−1). (B) MIC values of the tested materials against S. aureus, MRSA, E. coli, and E. coli GM. Error bars represent the standard deviation from three repeated experiments. (C) Fold change in MIC ([MIC]20/[MIC]1) of penicillin, polymyxin B, vancomycin, and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs against E. coli and S. aureus acclimated for 20 passages. [MIC]20 and [MIC]1 represent the MIC values at passage 20 and the first passage, respectively. Error bars represent the standard deviation from three repeated experiments, and the asterisks (C) denote a significant difference (*p < 0.05 and ***p < 0.001) compared with CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs group.

Evaluation of the antibacterial mechanism of DEX/ADA-CNGs, Cur@DEX/ADA-CNGs, and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs. (A) TEM images of MRSA (107 CFU mL−1) treated with DEX/ADA-CNGs, Cur@DEX/ADA-CNGs, and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs (100 μg mL−1) for 1 h. (B) Bright field and fluorescence images of MRSA (107 CFU mL−1) under excitation with blue light (460–480 nm) and green light (510–530 nm) after treated with DEX/ADA-CNGs, Cur@DEX/ADA-CNGs, and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs (100 μg mL−1) for 1 h, and stained with Baclight bacterial viability kit. Scale bars in (B) are 50 μm.

Evaluation of antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs (A) Antioxidant assays (DCFH-DA assay) of ARPE-19 cells without (Ctrl) and with H2O2 before being treated with DEX/ADA-CNGs, Cur@DEX/ADA-CNGs or CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs. (a) fluorescence microscopic images and (b) quantified results. (B) Quantitation of (a) IL-1β and (b) TNF-α secreted from ARPE-19 cells without LPS induction (Ctrl) and with LPS-induced inflammation before treatment with DEX/ADA-CNGs, Cur@DEX/ADA-CNGs, and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs. The concentration of materials was 10 μg mL−1. Error bars represent the standard deviation from three repeated experiments, and the asterisks in (A) and (B) denotes a significant difference (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001) compared with positive controls (H2O2 and LPS, respectively).

Therapeutic efficacy of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs in in vivo EO model. (A) Slit lamp microscopic images of S. aureus-induced EO in rats treated with PBS, vancomycin, DEX/ADA-CNGs or CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs (vancomycin: 1.0 mg mL−1; DEX/ADA-CNGs, CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs: 0.1 mg mL−1) 7 days after treatment. (B) Quantification results of clinical inflammation score. The purple dash line denotes the values for clinical inflammation score in the preoperative eye. An asterisk in (B) indicates statistically significant differences (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, and ***p < 0.001) for the mean value of quantified data compared to the value of preoperative groups; # p < 0.01, ## p < 0.001, and ### p < 0.0001 vs PBS groups (compared only within each time point group). (C) Ultrasound images of rat eyes after 7 days post-treatment and (D) the quantified results of high-density vitreous area, and the asterisks in (D) denote a significant difference (*p < 0.05, ***p < 0.001) compared with PBS group. The red arrows, yellow dotted lines, and white rectangle in (C) indicate high-density of fibrinous exudates in the anterior chamber and vitreous cavity, and thickness of the iris, respectively. Error bars represent the standard deviation from three repeated measurements. Scale bar in (A): 2 mm.

Histological analysis of the vitreous after treating S. aureus-induced EO with CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs. (A) H&E staining, (B) colonies of bacteria strains isolated from vitreous humor, (a) photographs of bacteria on agar plates and (b) the quantified data. The asterisks in (b) denote a significant difference (**p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001) compared with PBS group. (C) Gram-staining images of bacteria in the mid-vitreous of the EO eyes after treatment. The black arrows indicate bacterial colonies. Scale bars in (A) and (C) are 500 and 100 μm, respectively. Error bars represent the standard deviation from three repeated experiments. R: retina, OD: optic disc, L: lens, C: cornea, and blue rectangles in (A) denoteinfiltrated cell-rich region.
Schematic representation of the preparation of Cur-CQDs loaded DEX/ADA-CNGs (Cur-CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs) and their application in the treatment of endophthalmitis (EO) model.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Infectious endophthalmitis (EO) is a severe eye condition characterized by inflammation of the eye’s internal fluids and tissues, often resulting from infection by bacteria or fungi. Patients with EO typically experience reduced vision, pain, and conjunctival infection. Despite the use of antibiotics, challenges such as inflammation and drug resistance remain. In this work, we have developed dextran/aliphatic diamines carbonized nanogels (DEX/ADA-CNGs) as board-spectrum antimicrobial agents by a one-step mild carbonization procedure. The CNGs exhibit not only antimicrobial properties but also show good drug delivery potential. Curcumin-based carbon quantum dots (Cur-CQDs) as an anti-inflammatory agent are loaded into DEX/ADA-CNGs to form hybrid carbon-in-carbon nanocomposites (Cur-CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs), showing combined antimicrobial, antioxidative, and anti-inflammatory activities. Impressively, CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs maintain strong antibacterial effectiveness against drug-resistant strains, with minimum inhibitory concentrations below 110 µg mL−1. The Cur-CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs have demonstrated high biocompatibility both in vitro and in vivo. In a rat model of EO, they rapidly (less than 5 days) reduce microbial infection and inflammation in the eye. Given these promising results, Cur-CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs show great potential as an enduring intravitreal treatment option for infectious EO.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Endophthalmitis (EO) with common symptoms of ocular pain, redness, photophobia, diminished visual acuity, and hypopyon represents one of the most severe pathologies currently confronting the field of ophthalmology [1]. Defined as an inflammation of the eye’s inner layers, EO is caused by the intraocular colonization of infectious microbes, leading to exudation within the intraocular fluids to affect various ocular tissues, including aqueous humor, lens, and vitreous body [1]. EO can be categorized into three primary types based on its etiology: post-operative, post-traumatic, and endogenous [1]. The incidence of EO has risen in correlation with the proliferation of advanced ophthalmic procedures like cataract surgery, intraocular lens transplantation, intraocular injections, corneal transplantation, and glaucoma surgery. Notably, EO incidence rate following cataract surgery, recorded from 2010 to 2020, varies between 0.015 % and 0.035 % [2]. Temporal onset varies depending on the type of EO; for example, acute and non-acute postoperative EO generally manifests within a week and several weeks, respectively, following surgery or trauma [3]. For patients undergoing glaucoma surgery, the latency period for postoperative EO can range from several weeks to years [3]. The rapid progression of acute EO can result in irreversible vision loss within 24–48 h, potentially affecting surrounding tissues as well. The causative bacterial strains differ based on the infection pathway; Gram-positive bacteria are however responsible for approximately 80 % of cases. Gram-negative bacteria constitute around 13 %, while molds and other pathogens account for the remaining 7 % [4].

The current standard of care for acute EO primarily relies on intravitreal injections of broad-spectrum antibiotics, such as vancomycin for Gram-positive bacteria and third-generation cephalosporins for Gram-negative bacteria [5]. Fourth-generation fluoroquinolones like moxifloxacin have the capacity to penetrate the ocular surface effectively and inhibit Gram-positive bacteria [6]. Studies indicate that topical administration of moxifloxacin at two-hour intervals for three days prior to surgery can achieve aqueous humor concentrations sufficient for EO prevention [7]. Nevertheless, this method encounters obstacles in ensuring sufficient drug bioavailability, necessitating frequent and high-dosage applications. Such a regimen increases the likelihood of side effects, including blurred vision, dizziness, and palpitations [7]. Additionally, the intraocular concentrations achieved through topical antibiotic treatments often prove insufficient in effectively mitigating EO symptoms in many cases, rendering this method a less favored approach in ocular surface drug administration.

Resistance to antibiotics further complicates the treatment of EO [8]. While vancomycin remains effective against Gram-positive bacteria causing EO, emerging evidence suggests resistance in Enterococcus faecium [9]. Additionally, EO cases involving Gram-negative bacteria displayed high resistance to both aminoglycoside and cephalosporin antibiotics, leading to therapeutic failure [10]. To address intraocular infection and minimize tissue damage, anti-inflammatory corticosteroids like dexamethasone are commonly used in combination with antibiotics [11]. These steroids are retinal non-toxic and contribute to managing inflammation effectively. Therefore, intravitreal antibiotics remain the cornerstone of EO treatment, often augmented by systemic antibiotics, anti-inflammatory drugs, or vitrectomy as deemed appropriate [12]. Nonetheless, antibiotic resistance poses a significant threat to the efficacy of current treatment regimens, increasing the risk of irreversible vision loss. Given these challenges, the quest for rapid and effective therapeutic agents for EO remains a high-priority research focus in ophthalmology.

In recent years, antibacterial carbon nanomaterials—including carbon nanotubes, graphene, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs)—have garnered considerable attention for their efficacy against drug-resistant bacterial infections [13–18]. Unlike traditional antibiotics, these nanomaterials employ multifaceted antibacterial mechanisms, diminishing the likelihood of resistance development. CQDs, for instance, exhibit a range of antibacterial actions, such as inducing oxidative stress, disrupting bacterial membranes, and promoting bacterial gene apoptosis [19–21]. The versatile nature of CQDs allows for surface modifications of their charge and functional groups, thereby enabling tailoring of their antibacterial properties [22–25]. Charged CQDs, either positive or negative, have shown enhanced abilities to induce bacterial disruption and oxidative stress compared to their neutral counterparts [26]. Moreover, hydrophobic hydrocarbon chains and positive surface charges on CQDs can be regulated to enhance bacterial binding and membrane disruption [27]. In addition to their antimicrobial properties, certain CQDs have been shown to possess inherent anti-inflammatory and antioxidative capabilities [28–32]. These CQDs exert their anti-inflammatory effects through mechanisms such as scavenging of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS), modulation of redox biological processes, and regulation of cytokine expression. Such multifaceted anti-inflammatory and antioxidative activities open up promising avenues for their use in the treatment of inflammatory diseases.

Carbon nanomaterials such as graphene oxide and porous carbon nanomaterials have emerged as a promising platform for drug delivery applications, leveraging their unique physicochemical properties, such as high surface area, tunable surface chemistry, and mechanical strength [33–35]. These materials can load various therapeutic agents, from small molecular drugs to large biomolecules such as proteins and nucleic acids, offering targeting and sustained release [33–35]. Despite these advantages, several limitations and challenges constrain their application in drug delivery. Preparing these nanomaterials can be a complex process, and scaling up production to meet clinical needs remains a significant hurdle. Standardization of their properties for consistent performance is another concern. The high costs associated with both production and purification make widespread clinical implementation less feasible. Environmental considerations also pose challenges; for instance, the ecological impact of low-degradable carbon nanomaterials is not fully understood, but there are concerns about potential toxicity in aquatic ecosystems. Furthermore, majority of the carbon nanomaterials have been used exclusively as drug carriers, without possessing any inherent therapeutic effects. Although carbon nanomaterials have demonstrated promising attributes for drug delivery applications, further research is needed to address these challenges for them to become a viable option in mainstream medical treatments.

In this study, we synthesized carbonized nanogels (CNGs) through a mild pyrolysis process using a blend of dextran (DEX) and aliphatic diamines (ADA). The streamlined mild pyrolysis method for generating these carbonized nanocarriers offers advantages in standardization and scalability compared to other methods used for producing carbon nanomaterials like graphene oxide and carbon nanotubes. Remarkably, these CNGs exhibit broad-spectrum antimicrobial activities, proving effective even against multi-drug resistant (MDR) bacteria. Additionally, when used as nanocarriers for curcumin-derived CQDs (Cur-CQDs), the hybrid nanomaterials achieve both antibacterial and anti-inflammatory effects even against methicillin-resistant bacteria like methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). Importantly, these polysaccharide-based CNGs demonstrate resilience against metabolic breakdown and excretion within biological systems, thereby enhancing drug bioavailability and reducing the likelihood of adverse side effects. To test the therapeutic efficacy of this carbon-in-carbon nanoformulation (Cur-CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs), we applied it to a rat model for the treatment of EO caused by Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) (Scheme 1 ). Interestingly, a single intravitreal injection of Cur-CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs outperformed vancomycin at a dose concentration ten times higher than the CNGs. Overall, our findings show that Cur-CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs, with their high biocompatibility, serve as promising long-acting therapeutic agents for treating EO.

Dextran (DEX, Mw = 6, 70, and 250 kDa), alkylamine (1, n-diamino alkane (DA), n = 2–12), curcumin, ethanol, sodium chloride, monopotassium phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, and potassium chloride were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Tryptic soy broth (TSB) and Luria broth (LB) were purchased from Bioshop (Burlington, Canada). Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin, and all cell culture media were purchased from Gibco BRL (Grand Island, NY, USA). Alamar blue reagent was purchased from BioSource (Camarillo, CA, USA). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; containing 137 × 10−3 M NaCl, 5.0 × 10−3 M KCl, 0.5 × 10−3 M CaCl2, 1.0 × 10−3 M MgCl2, 10 × 10−3 M Na2HPO4, and 2.0 × 10−3 M KH2PO4; pH 7.4) was used to mimic physiological conditions. Milli-Q ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) from Millipore (Billerica, MA, USA) was used in all experiments. S. aureus (ATCC 25923), Escherichia coli (E. coli, BCRC 12438), MRSA and gentamicin-resistant E. coli (E. coli GM) were obtained from the Institute of Food Science (Hsinchu, Taiwan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of dextran/alkyl diamine carbon nanogels (DEX/ADA-CNGs)
ce:section.ce:para: Dextran (DEX, Mw = 6, 70 or 250 kDa, 20 mg) and alkyl diamines with different carbon chain lengths (carbon number: 4–12, 20 mg) were added into 1 mL of aqueous solutions in glass sample vials and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. The product obtained was cooled to ambient temperature and dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water before being sonicated for 2 h. The CNGs suspended solutions were then centrifuged at a relative centrifugal force (RCF) of 500 g for 30 min at 4 °C to remove the large particles, and the supernatants containing the DEX/ADA-CNGs were stored at 4 °C for later use.

Cur-CQDs were synthesized following the reported method [36]. Curcumin powder (200 mg) was heated in a glass sample vial at 210 °C for 2 h, and the brown product obtained was dispersed in sodium phosphate buffer solution (20 mL, 0.2 M, pH 12) before being subjected to sonication for 1 h and centrifugation at a RCF of 35,000 g for 1 h at 4 °C to remove larger particles. The supernatant was dialyzed (dialysis membrane MWCO 0.5–1.0 kDa) against sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.4, 10 mM) for 5 h and then against deionized water for 18 h before being stored at 4 °C for future use.

The DEX/ADA-CNGs lyophilized product (20 mg) was mixed separately with different amounts of Cur-CQD solutions (2.0, 4.0, or 10.0 mg mL−1, 5 mL), which were then sonicated for 2 h to form Cur-CQDs-loaded DEX/ADA-CNGs (referred to as CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs). Each of the CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNG solutions was then centrifuged (500 g, 4 °C) for 30 min to remove large aggregates, and the supernatant was centrifuged again at 35,000 g for 30 min to remove the supernatant, and the resulting precipitate was purified twice with sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.4, 10 mM) before being stored for future use.

The antibacterial activity of carbonized nanomaterials and nanocomposites (i.e., DEX/ADA-CNGs, Cur-CQDs and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs) was evaluated separately using Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, including S. aureus, MRSA, E. coli, and E. coli GM. Bacteria strains were incubated in LB medium at 37 °C overnight to reach ca. 5 × 108 CFU mL−1, which were then centrifuged at RCF of 3,000 g for 5 min to remove the medium and washed thrice with sodium phosphate buffer solution (5 mM, pH 7.4) before use. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of different carbonized nanomaterials and nanocomposites against the bacteria strains was confirmed by the broth microdilution method. Briefly, bacteria (104 CFU mL−1) was incubated with different concentrations of carbonized nanomaterials or nanocomposites (10–2–10–6 mg mL−1) in 5 mM sodium phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4) at 37 °C and 250 rpm for 1 h. LB medium was then added to each of the mixtures before being incubated at 37 °C for 18 h. For solid culture, the reaction mixture was serially diluted 107 times with LB medium. Aliquots (0.1 mL) of the diluted mixture was spread onto LB-agar plates, which was then incubated for 18 h to determine the number of colonies.

The MIC values of penicillin, vancomycin, polymyxin B, and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs against E. coli and S. aureus were determined following the method described in Section 2.5. S. aureus or E. coli (104 CFU mL−1) were incubated separately with the CNGs or antibiotics in LB broth at a concentration half of their MIC values. These mixtures were acclimated with the aforementioned materials at 37 °C for 24 h and new MIC values were determined. The test was repeated every day for 20 passages to investigate the development of drug resistance.

The interaction of bacteria with the carbonized nanomaterials or nanocomposites was explored by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The bacterial solution incubated at 37 °C overnight was centrifuged at 3,000 g for 5 min and washed three times with sodium phosphate buffer solution (5 mM, pH 7.4) before use. 107 CFU mL−1 bacteria solution and nanomaterials or nanocomposites were incubated in 5 mM sodium phosphate buffer solution for 1 h, which were centrifuged at 3,000 g for 5 min, washed three times with sodium phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4), and then incubated with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 1 h for fixing the bacterial cells. The fixed cells were washed three times with sodium phosphate buffer solution (5 mM, pH 7.4) and 15 μL of the sample was dropped on copper grid for TEM and on silicon wafer for SEM measurements. To observe the bacterial membrane changes induced by the nanomaterials or nanocomposites over different periods of time, Live/Dead BacLight Bacterial viability kit was used. Bacteria (107 CFU mL−1) incubated with nanomaterials or nanocomposites in 5 mM sodium phosphate buffer solution for 10, 30 and 60 min, respectively, were centrifuged at RCF of 3,000 g for 5 min and washed thrice with 5 mM sodium phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4), before being stained with Live/Dead BacLight bacterial viability reagent for 15 min. Each of the solution was washed twice with 5 mM sodium phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4) and dropped onto a glass slide for fluorescence microscopic measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic effect of Cur-CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs in rat EO model
ce:section.ce:para: For all the in vivo experiments, the animal use protocols were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Chang Gung University (Approval Number: CGU111-070). All animal procedures were performed in accordance with the ARVO Statement for the Use of Animals in Ophthalmic and Vision Research. SD (Sprague-Dawley) rats (24, divided into four groups, six rats in each group) were used to induce EO and evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of nanocomposites. Rat EO model was induced following the previous literature [37]. A 30G needle was used to inject 100 CFU of S. aureus into the vitreous of rats. After 24 h of induction, 10 μL of the materials (DEX/ADA-CNGs, CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs, and vancomycin) were injected into the vitreous cavity of rats, and the ocular inflammation was observed with a slit lamp microscope. Rat eyes receiving only PBS served as a control group. After sacrificing the rats after 7 days, a part of the vitreous body was removed and cultured on a plate to determine the number of bacteria in the eye, and the tissue was lysed to measure the expression of inflammatory factors in the cornea. In addition, a part of the duplicate group tissues was sectioned to evaluate the number of infiltrating cells and colonies in the tissues by H&E staining and Gram staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of nanogel structures after mild pyrolysis of DEX/ADA mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: Dextran (DEX) is a naturally branched polysaccharide made up of glucose molecules with α 1,6-glycosidic linkages that feature a wide molecular weight distribution, spanning from 3 kDa (kDa) to 40 megadaltons (MDa). Notably, DEX molecules with a molecular weight below 5 MDa are highly soluble in water [38]. Due to its high biocompatibility, low cost, and easy modification, DEX has been widely used in the field of biomedical applications, such as blood volume expanders, antithrombotic agents, drug carriers, coating materials for medical devices, and tissue engineering materials [39,40]. In this study, a straightforward, rapid, and high-yield synthesis of carbon nanogels (DEX/ADA-CNGs) was achieved in a single step by simply heating a mixture of DEX (MW 70 kDa) and one of the aliphatic diamines (ADA) with different carbon lengths (C n ; n = 4–12; 1,4-diaminobutane, 1,6-diaminohexane, 1,8-diaminooctane, 1,10-diaminodecane, and 1,12-diaminododecane) at 180 °C for 3 h. The pale yellow pyrolysis products have good water dispersibility, except DEX/ADA(1,12)-CNGs (Fig. S1A, Supporting Information). TEM images show an increase in the size of the CNGs formed with an increase in the chain length of the ADA. Their size distribution becomes greater for the materials prepared from the mixtures containing C10 and C12, which may be attributed to greater hydrophobicity and crosslinking length between DEX chains (Fig. S1B). The average sizes of DEX/ADA(1,n)-CNGs (n = 4, 6, 8, 10, 12) as measured from the TEM images are 209.8 ± 20.3, 257.4 ± 16.7, 309.2 ± 19.8, 259.4 ± 39.6 and 467.0 ± 73.8 nm, respectively, which are in good agreement with the results of 231.9 ± 22.3, 288.6 ± 35.3, 327.1 ± 46.8, 285.4 ± 59.6 and 414.0 ± 63.1 nm, respectively, obtained from dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements (Table S1, Supporting Information). As a control, DEX alone heated at 180 °C for 3 h yielded a transparent aqueous solution, with no colloidal particle formation observed under TEM (data not shown), revealing that ADA play a vital role in the condensation of DEX and their further carbonization.

During the heating of a mixture of DEX and ADA at 180 °C, the former undergoes oxidation and carbonization, during which aldehydes and carbonyl groups are formed. The aldehyde group of the oxidized DEX and the primary amine of the ADA react to form Schiff base-like structures, and crosslinked supramolecular structures via reversible condensation reaction, which then undergoes carbonization to form CNGs. Carbonization has been shown to result in the formation of nitrogen-doped heteroaromatics consisting of graphene-like domains embedded in crosslinked DEX nanogel structures [41]. The absorption spectrum of DEX/ADA-CNGs (i.e., DEX70k/DA(1,10)-CNGs; MW of DEX: 70 kDa) shows a very broad absorption band centered at 300 nm contributed by conjugated π → π* transitions (C = C bonds) (Fig. 1 A). The CNGs exhibit fluorescence properties at an excitation/emission wavelengths of 365/475 nm (Fig. 1B). The fluorescence microscopic images show granular structure of the CNGs (Figs. S2 and S3, Supporting Information), and the fluorescence intensity of CNGs depends on the carbon chain length of the ADA and the molecular weight of DEX.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of DEX/ADA-CNGs show characteristic peaks at ca. 1150, 1384, and 3400 cm−1, corresponding to C–N stretch, C–O–H bending, and O–H/N–H stretch, respectively, revealing that condensation of ADA and DEX is involved in the formation of CNGs (Fig. 1C). A prominent stretching peak at 1560 cm−1, which is characteristic of aromatic CC bonds is a clear indicator of the carbonization process, suggesting the formation of aromatic structures within the CNGs. Additionally, the presence of a CN stretching peak at 1670 cm−1 is indicative of Schiff base formation, a type of condensation reaction where the amino groups of ADA react with the aldehyde or ketone groups of oxidized DEX. The XRD pattern of DEX/ADA-CNGs and Cur-CQDs revealed a characteristic broad band at approximately 20–25°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of a graphene-like structure with an interplanar spacing of approximately 0.32 nm (Fig. S4, Supporting Information) [42]. This feature indicates the formation of disordered graphitic domains within the carbonized structure of the nanogels and Cur-CQDs. Additionally, the XRD pattern of DEX/ADA-CNGs exhibited two other distinct bands centered at approximately 34° and 45°, which can be attributed to the residual molecular crystal structure of DEX [43]. These bands suggest partial retention of the DEX's original crystalline form within the nanogel matrix.

The nitrogen-doped graphene-like domains in the rigid crosslinking DEX nanogels endow the photoluminescence (PL) properties of the nanogels (Fig. S2) [44]. In addition, the confined-domain crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) greatly promotes the generation of PL in carbonized nanomaterials [45,46]. Generally, electron-abundant heteroatom functional groups in the graphene-like domains tend to return to their ground states from excited states through non-radiative pathways, and hence they do not exhibit PL. By contrast, in CEE their movement and rotation are restricted through formation of cross-linked polymers. This limitation boosts the radiative transition while reducing the non-radiative transition, consequently amplifying the PL of the sub-fluorophores (electron-abundant heteroatom functional groups) in the CNGs [45]. A negligible fluorescence was observed for DEX/ADA(1,4) mainly because their loose nanogel matrix (Fig. S1B) could not strongly restrict the vibrations and rotations of sub-fluorophores in the CNGs.

The as-prepared DEX6k/DA(1,10)-CNGs, DEX70kDA(1,10)-CNGs, and DEX250kDA(1,10)-CNGs are all spherical particles with PL properties (Fig. S3) and their average sizes measured by TEM are 75.2 ± 46.1, 273.7 ± 131.9, and 285.2 ± 92.2 nm (Fig. S5, Supporting Information), respectively. As expected, the average size of DEX/DA(1,10)-CNGs is larger upon increasing the molecular weight of DEX. The quantum yields (QYs) of all prepared DEX/DA-CNGs are less than 5 % using quinine sulfate (QY = 54 % in 0.5 M H2SO4) as a standard. The low QYs of DEX/DA-CNGs are mainly due to abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carbonyl groups (oxygen atomic ratio >20 % as determined by elemental analysis) in the CNGs. Oxygen atoms in the functional groups on the photoluminescent carbon dots can act as electron or energy acceptors to dissipate the energy through a non-radiative process [47]. In addition, oxygen-containing functional groups can change the energy levels of the electronic states in carbon dots, potentially leading to greater opportunities through non-radiative decay paths [48].

Polysaccharides have been widely used as carriers for drug delivery, and drug-loaded DEX have been reported to increase the biocompatibility and enhance the stability of the drug in biological systems [49]. Of the various DEX/ADA-CNGs synthesized, DEX70k/ADA(1,10)-CNGs has the highest (ca. 71 %) production yield, and hence it was used to load Cur-CQDs (hereafter the as-prepared CNGs are referred to CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs) to impart antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties for subsequent applications. The average size determined from TEM images shows that compared with the CNGs carrier, CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs (Cur-CQDs:DEX/ADA-CNGs = 0.5:1, w/w) have a smaller average particle size (253.5 ± 13.2 nm) (Table S1). We note that the shape of the particles did not change significantly before and after Cur-CQDs loading (Fig. 1D). The smaller size of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs may be due to the multiple electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and π–π stacking among CQDs and CNGs, which affects the swelling properties of CNGs. The zeta potential (ζ) values of DEX/ADA-CNGs and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs are +18.3 ± 3.5 and +3.6 ± 1.7 mV, respectively. The decrease in ζ supports the binding of DEX/ADA-CNGs with negative charged Cur-CQDs (ζ = −37.7 ± 1.3 mV). In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectra and the fluorescence spectra show that Cur-CQDs were successfully loaded onto CNGs. The absorption band at around 285 nm observed in the Cur-CQDs is attributed to the π → π* transitions of conjugated CC bonds, which indicates the presence of graphitic carbon domains [36]. Additionally, the shoulder at 320 nm is associated with the n → π* transitions of CO bonds. When Cur-CQDs are loaded into DEX/ADA-CNGs to form CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs, the absorption intensity at 285 nm decreases compared to that of the pure Cur-CQDs, which indicates the successful incorporation of Cur-CQDs into the nanogel matrix. Compared with DEX/ADA-CNGs, the characteristic absorption peak of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs shows a red shift to 400–500 nm (Fig. 1A) attributed to the π-π stacking interactions and multiple electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions between the functional groups of the Cur-CQDs and the CNGs. The reduction in fluorescence intensity of the composite further confirms the successful encapsulation of Cur-CQDs within the DEX/ADA-CNGs nanogel matrix (Fig. 1B). Specifically, the decrease in fluorescence can be explained by the occurrence of charge transfer- or energy transfer-mediated fluorescence quenching mechanisms that are activated due to the close proximity and interaction of Cur-CQDs with the DEX/ADA-CNGs [50].

The bright field microscopic images display spherical CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs (Fig. S6A, Supporting Information). Compared with DEX/ADA-CNGs, the blue and green fluorescence images of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs are both weakened, which are in good agreement with the result observed by the fluorescence spectroscopy. The result also suggests that the CQDs were encapsulated in the CNGs. CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs still retain the functional groups derived from CQDs and CNGs such as N–H/O–H, CO, C–O–H, C–N, and aromatic CC/C–C as observed from the FTIR spectra (Fig. 1C), supporting a successful loading of the CQDs onto the DEX/ADA-CNGs. We further synthesized CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs with different ratios of Cur-CQDs (Cur-CQDs:DEX/ADA-CNGs = 0.5:1, 1:1, 2.5:1; w/w). An increase in the amount of Cur-CQDs resulted in the aggregation of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs as evident from the TEM images (Fig. S6B), which may be due to the strong interaction between CQDs and CNGs that leads to the formation of CQDs-loaded CNGs via mutual adhesion. The loading efficiencies of Cur-CQDs onto CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs at different weight ratios of Cur-CQDs:DEX/ADA-CNGs (0.5:1, 1:1, 2.5:1) were determined through fluorescence measurements to be 53.7 ± 4.2 %, 71.6 ± 5.7 %, and 72.8 ± 3.6 %, respectively (refer to the Supporting Information for details).

We further evaluated the CQDs release profile of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs in artificial tears (ATS) for 120 h by measuring the fluorescence of the supernatant (containing Cur-CQDs) at regular intervals. The cumulative release of Cur-CQDs from the CNGs was 50 % within 1 h and increased to ∼ 70 % in 72 h as shown in Fig. S7A (Supporting Information), which is closely fixed with Korsmeyer–Peppas model. The log cumulative percent drug released vs log time between 0.25–3 h and 3–72 h were plotted in Fig. S7B and C. Based on the equation: log (M t/M ∞) = log k m + n·log t; M t/M ∞ is the fraction of drug released at time t, km is a constant incorporating structural and geometric characteristics of the drug-polymer system, t is the release time, and n is the release exponent. Depending on the value of n, the drug release mechanism can be classified as Fickian diffusion (n ≤ 0.43), non-Fickian or anomalous transport (0.43 < n < 0.85), case-II transport or zero-order release (n ≈ 0.89), or super case II transport (n > 0.85) [51]. The Korsmeyer–Peppas exponent (n) of the CQDs was determined to be 0.46 and 0.03 before and after 3 h, respectively. Between 0.25–3 h, CQDs were released from DEX/ADA-CNGs by non-Fickian or anomalous transport, indicating an overlapping of different phenomena during release driven by drug diffusion and nanogel swelling. After 3 h, Cur-CQDs released from DEX/ADA-CNGs is approached by Fickian diffusion. The potential of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs for enhanced bioavailability is significant [51], particularly given their ability to sustain the release of Cur-CQDs at lower concentrations, which could potentially reduce dosing frequency and improve patient compliance. This slow-release mechanism is crucial for therapeutic applications where extended drug availability can enhance treatment efficacy while minimizing systemic side effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Superior antimicrobial, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory activities of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial efficiencies of DEX/ADA-CNGs, Cur-CQDs, and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs were determined by serial dilution method [27]. The minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) of DEX/ADA-CNGs toward S. aureus, MRSA, E. coli, and E. coli GM are 12.5 ± 2.6, 4.9 ± 0.8, 10.0 ± 3.2 and 9.1 ± 1.7 µg mL−1, respectively, while Cur-CQDs exhibited negligible antibacterial activity toward the four tested bacterial strains (Fig. 2 ). The MICs of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs (Cur-CQDs:DEX/ADA-CNGs = 0.5:1) against various bacterial strains—S. aureus, MRSA, E. coli, and E. coli GM—were determined to be 96.4 ± 10.7, 9.8 ± 5.4, 91.8 ± 2.6 and 102.9 ± 17.3 µg mL−1, respectively. Notably, the CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs exhibited a lower MIC value toward MRSA, which could be attributed to the antibacterial effect of Cur-CQDs also being exerted on this strain. Increase in the ratio of Cur-CQDs in CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs (Cur-CQDs:DEX/ADA-CNGs = 1:1, 2.5:1; w/w) showed no significant antibacterial effect on the four bacterial strains at maximum tested concentration of 200 µg mL−1 (Fig. S8, Supporting Information). The significant decline in antibacterial activity of DEX/ADA-CNGs after the massive loading of Cur-CQDs may be caused by a large number of Cur-CQDs capped on the CNGs’ surfaces, thereby affecting their interactions with bacterial membranes.

TEM images of MRSA after interactions with the three types of CNGs are presented in Fig. 3 A. The interaction of DEX/ADA-CNGs with MRSA resulted in the aggregation of bacteria and the disruption of their membranes, leading to bacterial cell death. However, the MRSA bacterial membrane integrity was relatively intact with slight aggregation after incubation with CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs. The results of Baclight Live/dead staining assay (Fig. 3B) support the result obtained from the TEM images. Like DEX/ADA-CNGs, the curcumin molecules-loaded DEX/ADA-CNGs (Cur@DEX/ADA-CNGs) (0.5:1; w/w) caused aggregation of bacteria, as evident from the TEM image and Baclight Live/dead staining assay. The MIC values of Cur@DEX/ADA-CNGs toward S. aureus, MRSA, E. coli, and E. coli GM were determined to be 133.6 ± 5.4, 93.9 ± 5.9, 142.8 ± 3.6, and 99.9 ± 7.4 µg mL−1, respectively (Fig. 2B). The limited antibacterial activity of curcumin is a contributing factor, resulting in Cur@DEX/ADA-CNGs demonstrating lower antibacterial effects compared to CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs.

Drug-resistance development is an important issue in antibacterial field, and hence, the potential of E. coli and S. aureus to develop resistance to CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs were evaluated by long-term co-culture with half the MIC of the material every day for 20 passages, and compared with antibiotics, such as penicillin, vancomycin, and polymyxin B. After 20 passages, the MIC values were determined and compared with the original MIC value. The MIC of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs group showed no significant change even after 20 passages in both E. coli and S. aureus. However, in the antibiotic-treated groups, such as penicillin, vancomycin, and polymyxin B, the MIC values increased by 106.1-/64.3-, 120.2-/43.7-, and 2.9-/1.9-fold for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. Both penicillin and vancomycin exhibit antibacterial activity by inhibiting the synthesis of the bacterial cell wall. The penicillins interfere with the last step in peptidoglycan synthesis by acting as an analog of the D-Ala-D-Ala portion of the N-acetyl muramic moiety [52]. Vancomycin inhibits the synthesis of bacterial cell wall phospholipids and peptidoglycan polymerization by binding with the N-acyl-D-Ala-D-Ala portion in the second step of synthesis [53]. Polymyxins, the cationic lipopeptide antibiotics that target the bacterial membrane and disrupt them causing lethality, are considered to be the last-line of antibiotics available at the moment [54]. Wide use of antibiotics causes bacteria to develop resistance by different pathways. For example, bacteria acquisition of plasmids that encode β-lactamase or from chromosomal mutations to survive from the penicillin, altered peptidoglycan terminus (D-Ala-D-Lac instead of the usual D-Ala-D-Ala) against vancomycin, or change the lipopolysaccharide (LPS) structure to avoid polymyxin binding [55–57]. However, the CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs did not induce drug resistance development even after 20 passages.

Our results showed that DEX/ADA-CNGs possess robust broad-spectrum antibacterial properties, whereas the incorporation of Cur-CQDs into the nanocomposites slightly reduced their antibacterial efficiency. However, the overarching benefits of Cur-CQDs, primarily their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, still render them beneficial in the EO model in vivo. Consequently, CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs prepared from a specific formulation ratio of CQDs to CNGs (0.5:1) was selected for further research, balancing antibacterial efficacy with additional therapeutic effects. Addressing inflammation and oxidative stress is crucial in EO treatment. Curcumin, a natural antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agent, is often limited by its poor water solubility [58]. However, by carbonizing curcumin under optimal conditions, we were able to produce Cur-CQDs with not only improving water dispersibility but also increasing biological efficacy [36].

We further performed the stability tests on DEX/ADA-CNGs, assessing their antibacterial activity after storage at 4 °C for three months. The results from these tests indicate that the antibacterial efficacy of the aged DEX/ADA-CNGs remains comparable to that of freshly prepared samples. This consistent performance demonstrates the robustness of the CNGs under prolonged storage conditions. Additionally, we have monitored the physical stability of both the DEX/ADA-CNGs and the composite by measuring changes in their hydrodynamic size and zeta potential over the same period. These parameters serve as indicators of colloidal stability, including the propensity for particle aggregation. Our findings show no significant changes in either the hydrodynamic size or zeta potential, suggesting that there is no notable aggregation or degradation of the nanogels and composite.

We then assessed the biological efficacy of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs against oxidative and inflammatory responses induced by H2O2 and LPS in human retinal pigment epithelia (ARPE-19) cells by DCFH-DA staining and ELISA kits for IL-1β and TNF-α, respectively. The results revealed that while DEX/ADA-CNGs alone had negligible antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects at 10 μg mL−1, the loading of Cur-CQDs notably enhanced these properties in the composite (Fig. 4 ). The enhanced antioxidant capabilities of Cur-CQDs are likely due to their graphite-like carbon core enriched with a high density of phenolic groups on the surface, aiding in electron transfer [36]. Cur-CQDs may mitigate inflammation, primarily through their impact on key cellular signaling pathways and the modulation of inflammation-related molecules [59]. The anti-inflammatory effects of Cur-CQDs are largely attributed to curcumin moieties retained on the surface of CQDs, which are known for their broad biological activity. Cur-CQDs modulate the inflammatory response by influencing various signaling pathways involved in the activation and production of pro-inflammatory mediators. Specifically, curcumin is known to inhibit the activity of transcription factors like NF-κB, which plays a central role in the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1β and TNF-α. The anti-inflammatory efficacy of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs is clearly demonstrated in the reduced (over 45 %) inflammatory cytokines, IL-1β, and TNF-α. By suppressing NF-κB activation, Cur-CQDs reduce the secretion of these cytokines, effectively mitigating the inflammatory response. Additionally, the temperature-assisted fragmentation of curcumin leads to the formation of guaiacol derivatives. These derivatives have been shown to significantly bolster antioxidant defense and stress response, activating key elements like redox-regulated transcription factor Nrf2 and paraoxonase [60]. Moreover, both Cur-CQDs and guaiacol derivatives exhibit potent anti-inflammatory activities, primarily through the downregulation of IL-6 [61]. This finding not only underscores the potential of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs in antimicrobial applications but also highlights the synergistic effects of carbonized nanomaterials derived from polyphenols and polysaccharides. Such combinations could pave the way for more effective treatments in EO, simultaneously tackling bacterial infections and mitigating inflammatory responses. In addition, under conditions simulating sunlight exposure, these nanomaterials do not generate significant levels of ROS, including singlet oxygen (Fig. S9, Supporting Information). These findings are critical in establishing the safety profile of our treatment approach, particularly when considering the delicate nature of ocular tissues.

To assess the viability of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs for ocular treatment, their in vitro biocompatibility was first tested using ARPE-19 cells. The MTT assay indicated that these nano-composites were not significantly toxic to ARPE-19 cells, even at a concentration of 100 μg mL−1, which is tenfold higher than their effective concentration (Fig. S10A, Supporting Information). This finding is significant as it suggests a favorable safety profile for these nanocomposites in cellular environments. Following these encouraging in vitro results, our focus shifted to in vivo biocompatibility, which was examined through intravitreal injections of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs into rat eyes. This stage is critical in confirming the suitability of these nanocomposites for direct application in living organisms. Maintaining a sterile environment in the animal testing area is paramount to ensure the integrity of the results, with regular disinfection protocols in place to protect against external contamination. The observations from slit lamp microscopy reveal no adverse tissue effects in rats treated with DEX/ADA-CNGs and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs over a period of 14 days (Fig. S10B). Moreover, the intraocular pressure measurements in these rats remained stable and within the normal physiological range of 9–15 mm Hg both before and after the injections, a vital aspect in ruling out any pressure-related ocular issues (Fig. S10C). Further detailed analysis using slit lamp microscopy indicated that the ocular surface and surrounding tissues, including the conjunctiva, iris, and lens, were clear and unaffected by the treatment. Crucially, there was no observable difference in the sclera and cornea between the CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs-treated and untreated (PBS) groups.

The hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining images of the cornea of the DEX/ADA-CNGs- and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs-treated groups were similar to that of the control (untreated, PBS) group (Fig. S11A, Supporting Information). The three different layers of the cornea, the epithelium, stroma, and endothelium were clear and intact. The epithelium was typically stratified, the stroma appeared as a densely packed layer, and the endothelium, which is a single layer of cells on the inner surface of the cornea could be clearly distinguished. Similarly, the staining images of the retina after treating with DEX/ADA-CNGs or CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs also revealed the organization and morphology of several distinct layers similar to the control group (Fig. S11B), including, the inner plexiform layer, inner nuclear layer, outer plexiform layer, outer nuclear layer, outer limiting membrane, and retinal pigment epithelium, facilitating the identification of cellular elements within each layer. No infiltration of cells or signs of inflammation was observed, which reveals the biocompatibility of the CNGs. The absence of apoptosis in the eye tissues (cornea and retina) was further confirmed by TUNEL staining (Fig. S11C and D). The absence of TUNEL-positive cells in both groups confirms the lack of significant DNA fragmentation, as would have been detected by the TUNEL assay. These results collectively underscore good biocompatibility of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs, both in vitro and in vivo, illustrating their promising potential for intraocular applications. The findings from these comprehensive assessments are crucial, demonstrating the capability of these nanocomposites to be used safely in sensitive biological environments like the human eye without inducing adverse effects. This opens up new possibilities for their application in treating various ocular conditions, especially those requiring direct delivery of therapeutic agents to the eye, marking a significant advancement in ocular therapeutics.

The initial findings regarding the antibacterial, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory properties, along with the biocompatibility of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs, lay a solid foundation for further research. The next steps involve comprehensive biocompatibility testing involving a broader range of cell types relevant to ocular tissues in the future. This will help confirm their safety across different biological systems, particularly for ocular applications where sensitivity is paramount. Extended toxicity studies will also be essential to assess long-term safety and identify any potential adverse effects that might arise from chronic use. Moreover, pharmacokinetic and biodistribution studies will also be conducted to understand how CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs distribute within the body, metabolize, and are excreted. These studies are critical for determining optimal dosing schedules and delivery methods, which are pivotal for maximizing therapeutic efficacy while minimizing side effects. Once these parameters are well-defined and safety is established, clinical trials can commence to evaluate the effectiveness of these nanocomposites in human subjects, targeting specific ocular conditions. Lastly, mechanistic studies will delve deeper into the pathways through which CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs exert their biological effects. Understanding the molecular mechanisms involved in their antioxidant activity and immune modulation will provide insights into how these properties can be optimized. Alongside scientific exploration, engaging with regulatory bodies will ensure that all legal and ethical considerations are addressed, paving the way for the transition of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs from laboratory research to clinical use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced therapeutic efficacy in treating EO
ce:section.ce:para: An in vivo EO model was induced in the rat eye following a previous report [37]. S aureus (100 CFU, 10 μL) was injected into the vitreous of the rat with a 30G needle to induce infection. After 12 h, DEX/ADA-CNGs (100 μg mL−1, 10 μL), CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs (100 μg mL−1, 10 μL), and vancomycin (1.0 mg mL−1, 10 μL; as a control) were separately administered by intravitreal injection (single injection). The changes in ocular manifestations were observed with a slit lamp microscope at regular intervals for 7 days. After the induction of EO, the vitreous body of the rats showed signs of thickening of the anterior chamber and vitreous opacity, showing that the bacteria had successfully infected and proliferated in the eye (Fig. 5 A). The vitreous of the CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs-treated group became slightly clearer than other groups after the third day of material injection, while hypopyon and vitreous opacity were greatly improved in the CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs and DEX/ADA-CNGs groups after seven days. The severity score of EO was determined by dividing the eye into four parts: cornea, anterior chamber, vitreous body, and retina, with each part assigned a score of 0–3 according to the severity of symptoms, and the scores were finally summed up [37]. The PBS group maintained a very high score of 9–11 during the observation period, while the scores of vancomycin, DEX/ADA-CNGs, and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs on the seventh day were 4.3 ± 0.9, 2.8 ± 0.3, 1.5 ± 0.4, respectively (Fig. 5B). Compared with commercially available antibiotics (i.e., vancomycin), only 1/10 of the concentration of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs or DEX/ADA-CNGs was needed at the same injection frequency to show enhanced therapeutic effect. Furthermore, in comparison with DEX/ADA-CNGs, CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs improved the symptoms of EO more quickly. At 5 and 7 days, the score of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs was similar to that of uninfected eyes (purple dash line), which may be attributed to the antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects provided by Cur-CQDs.

For a thorough assessment of inflammatory changes, B-ultrasound, a standard diagnostic tool in clinical practice for managing EO [62], was employed to observe the whole eyeballs of rats after 7 days of treatment. The scan images showed a high-density of fibrinous exudate in the vitreous cavity (yellow dotted line) and anterior chamber (red arrow), and thickness of the iris (white rectangle) in the PBS and vancomycin-treated groups (Fig. 5C). The vitreous cavity, a substantial intraocular compartment posterior to the lens and anterior to the retina, is typically occupied by a transparent, gelatinous substance termed vitreous humor. The pathological deposition of fibrinous exudate within this cavity manifests as turbidity or opacification, thereby compromising visual clarity by inducing phenomena such as cloudiness or haziness. This accumulation disrupts the normal optical properties of the eye, potentially leading to significant visual impairment [63]. The accumulation of fibrinous exudate, a proteinaceous inflammatory byproduct, within this chamber precipitates a pathological state characterized by ocular inflammation, nociceptive discomfort, and potential disturbances in visual acuity [64]. The DEX/ADA-CNGs-treated group showed a reduction in the high-density vitreous area, however, the iris remained thick. The iris returned to normal, and significantly less area of high-density vitreous was observed in the CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs-treated group and was in line with the uninfected group (Fig. 5D), clearly demonstrating that the combined treatment not only reduces the bacterial load but also more effectively controls the inflammatory processes leading to exudate formation. Thus, the correlation between the reduction of fibrinous exudate and the treatment efficacy underscores the importance of using advanced therapeutic agents like CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs. By effectively controlling both infection and inflammation, these treatments hold promise for significantly improving outcomes in ocular inflammatory diseases, where maintaining visual clarity is paramount.

The H&E staining images showed intraocular inflammation and a large number of infiltrating cells in the PBS and vancomycin-treated groups (Fig. 6 A), which is one of the obvious signs of EO. In addition, the retinas displayed partial detachment, and fibrin occluded the anterior chambers. In contrast, well-preserved ocular structures were observed in the DEX/ADA-CNGs and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs-treated groups. The quantification of intraocular S. aureus after 7 days of treatment of EO shows ∼70 % and >85 % reduction in the DEX/ADA-CNGs- and CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs-treated group, respectively (Fig. 6B). In contrast to in vitro studies (Fig. 2), the CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs exhibited a superior antibacterial effect compared to DEX/ADA-CNGs. The slower release of Cur-CQDs from the DEX/ADA-CNGs may allow for a prolonged antibacterial effect, which is critical in treating persistent infections like EO. The Gram-staining images in Fig. 6C corroborate this, showing fewer bacterial colonies in samples treated with CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs compared to those treated with DEX/ADA-CNGs alone. A recent study revealed that curcumin suppresses inflammation by shifting macrophages from M1 to M2 polarization phenotype [65], it may contribute to the enhanced therapeutic effect of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs, compared to DEX/ADA-CNGs. Overall, these results paint a comprehensive picture of the therapeutic potential of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs in treating EO. The combined antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antibacterial properties of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs not only address the primary infection but also mitigate associated complications, making them as a promising candidate for addressing current clinical challenges in EO treatment. These nanocomposites address the dual challenges of EO by effectively combating bacterial infection while simultaneously mitigating the inflammatory processes that exacerbate the condition. Traditional treatment strategies often focus primarily on the microbial aspect without adequately addressing inflammation, which can continue to damage ocular tissues even after the infection is under control. Thus, the integrated approach provided by CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs offers a comprehensive solution that not only targets the pathogens but also preserves ocular function by reducing inflammatory damage.

Exploring combination therapies forms another crucial aspect of the research agenda. By combining CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs with other therapeutic agents such as antibiotics and immunomodulators, there is potential to enhance treatment efficacy for complex ocular diseases. This approach aims to leverage the multifunctional capabilities of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs to address the multifactorial nature of severe ocular infections, potentially reducing the burden of treatment and improving patient outcomes. To translate these findings into clinical applications, further research is essential. Detailed in vitro and in vivo studies are needed to comprehensively assess the safety profile of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs, focusing on potential side effects, biodistribution, and long-term effects of these nanomaterials. Such studies will help establish the optimal dosing regimens, administration routes, and treatment durations. Additionally, clinical trials should be designed to validate the efficacy and safety of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs in human subjects, comparing their performance against existing EO therapies to quantify improvements in treatment outcomes.

We synthesized CNGs having excellent antibacterial effects from DEX and 1,10-diaminodecane at 180 °C. The CNGs encapsulate Cur-CQDs derived from curcumin to form a stable carbonized nanocomposite, which exhibits antibacterial, antioxidative, and anti-inflammatory abilities. The Cur-CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs significantly alleviated the symptoms of S. aureus-induced infectious EO, in which the DEX/ADA-CNGs inhibit bacteria by wrapping them and disrupting the membrane. The enveloping ability of the CNGs also holds the potential to prevent the release of toxins upon bacterial rupture, thereby safeguarding the neighboring tissues from additional harm. On the other hand, the CQDs provide great antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. The slow-release ability of CNGs as a carrier can prolong the action time of Cur-CQDs in the eye. In vitro and in vivo test results showed that CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs exhibit good biocompatibility. In the in vivo EO model, the intravitreal administration of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs at just 1/10 of the concentration of commercially available antibiotic (vancomycin) resulted in a superior therapeutic effect, with significant reduction of the infiltrating cells in the vitreous. Our findings are promising, suggesting that CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs could be a highly effective treatment option for EO. This approach has the potential to address several shortcomings of current antibiotic therapies, including drug resistance, low biocompatibility, and inadequate antioxidative and anti-inflammatory activities, offering a more comprehensive treatment strategy. Looking ahead, we aim to delve deeper into understanding the in vivo metabolism-related pathways of CQDs@DEX/ADA-CNGs.
